saida_bruta:
“You look awfully familiar,” he remarked civilly. “Weren’t you with the First Division in the war?”

“Indeed. I served with the Twenty-eighth Infantry regiment.”

“And I was in the Sixteenth until June of nineteen-eighteen. I felt certain I’d encountered you somewhere.”

We conversed briefly regarding a few damp, dreary French hamlets. Apparently he resided nearby, as he mentioned he had recently purchased a motorboat and intended to test it early tomorrow.

“Care to join me, old sport? We’ll just skim along the coast of the Sound.”

“At what hour?”

“Whenever it is most convenient for you.”

I was just about to inquire what his name was when Jordan turned our way with a smile.

“Enjoying yourself more now?” she asked.

“A great deal.” I addressed my new companion once more. “This gathering is strange for me. I haven’t encountered our host yet. I reside next door—” I gestured toward the obscured boundary line far off, “and a driver was sent over by Gatsby with a written card.”

He stared at me momentarily, appearing slightly bewildered by my words.

“I am Gatsby,” he remarked abruptly.

“What!” I gasped. “Oh, please forgive me.”

“I assumed you were aware, old sport. I fear I make a rather poor host.”

saida_bruta:
“You seem incredibly familiar,” he noted politely. “Were you not with the First Division during the war?”

“Yes, I was. I served in the Twenty-eighth Infantry.”

“And I was with the Sixteenth until June of nineteen-eighteen. I felt positive I’d seen you somewhere before.”

We spoke momentarily about a couple of wet, dismal French villages. Evidently he lived close by, because he mentioned he’d just bought a motorboat and planned to try it out tomorrow morning.

“Would you like to come along, old sport? We can cruise along the Sound.”

“What time?”

“Any hour that suits you best.”

I was on the verge of asking his name when Jordan turned toward us, smiling.

“Having a better time now?” she inquired.

“Much better.” I turned back to my new acquaintance. “This party is unusual for me. I haven’t even met the host. I live right next door—” I waved toward the invisible property line in the distance, “and Gatsby sent a chauffeur over with an invitation.”

He looked at me for a second, seeming quite puzzled by what I said.

“I’m Gatsby,” he said suddenly.

“What!” I exclaimed. “Oh, I beg your pardon.”

“I thought you knew, old sport. I’m afraid I’m not a very good host.”

saida_bruta:
“You look remarkably familiar,” he remarked in a friendly tone. “Weren’t you with the First Division in the war?”

“Indeed, I was. I served in the Twenty-eighth Infantry.”

“And I was with the Sixteenth up until June of nineteen-eighteen. I felt certain I recognized you from somewhere.”

We conversed briefly about a few damp, gloomy French towns. Apparently he resided nearby, as he mentioned he’d recently purchased a motorboat and intended to test it tomorrow.

“Would you care to join me, old sport? We could take a run along the Sound.”

“At what time?”

“Whatever hour works best for you.”

I was just about to inquire his name when Jordan leaned over to us, beaming.

“Are you enjoying yourself more now?” she asked.

“Considerably more.” I faced back toward my new companion. “This gathering is rather strange for me. I haven’t even introduced myself to the host. I reside just next door—” I gestured toward the unseen boundary line far off, “and Gatsby dispatched a driver to deliver my invitation.”

He stared at me for a moment, appearing thoroughly bewildered by my remark.

“I’m Gatsby,” he announced abruptly.

“What!” I gasped. “Oh, please forgive me.”

“I assumed you were aware, old sport. I fear I make a rather poor host.”

“You seem incredibly familiar,” he noted with a warm smile. “Weren’t you part of the First Division during the war?”

“I certainly was. I served with the Twenty-eighth Infantry.”

“And I was stationed with the Sixteenth until June of nineteen-eighteen. I was positive I’d seen your face before.”

We chatted for a moment about some wet, dreary villages in France. Evidently he lived in the neighborhood, mentioning he had just bought a hydroplane and planned to try it out tomorrow.

“Would you like to come along, old sport? We could cruise around the Sound.”

“What time?”

“Any time that suits you best.”

I was on the verge of asking his name when Jordan leaned toward us with a bright expression.

“Are you having a better time now?” she inquired.

“Much better.” I turned back to my new acquaintance. “This party is quite unusual for me. I haven't even met the host yet. I live right next door—” I pointed toward the invisible property line in the distance, “and Gatsby sent a chauffeur over with an invitation.”

He gazed at me for a second, looking entirely perplexed by my comment.

“I’m Gatsby,” he said suddenly.

“What!” I exclaimed. “Oh, I beg your pardon.”

“I thought you knew, old sport. I’m afraid I’m a rather dreadful host.”

We talked for a short while about some wet, dreary French villages. It turned out he lived close by, mentioning he had just bought a hydroplane and planned to try it out tomorrow.

“Any time that suits you best.”

I was on the verge of asking who he was when Jordan looked our way with a bright smile.

“What!” I exclaimed. “Oh, I beg your pardon.”

“I thought you knew, old sport. I'm afraid I'm not a very good host.”

saida_bruta:
“You seem incredibly familiar,” he remarked, flashing a warm smile. “Weren’t you over in the Third Division throughout the Great War?”

“Yes, I was. I served in the Ninth Machine-gun Battalion.”

“And I was with the Seventh Infantry until June of nineteen-eighteen. I felt sure I’d recognized your face from somewhere.”

We spoke momentarily about some wet, dreary French towns. It turned out he was a neighbor, and he mentioned he had just bought a hydroplane and planned to try it tomorrow.

“Want to come along, old sport? We could go for a run along the Sound.”

“At what hour?”

“Any time that suits you.”

I was on the verge of asking his name when Jordan looked our way with a bright grin.

“Are you having a better time now?” she questioned.

“Much better.” I turned back to my acquaintance. “This party is quite unusual for me. I haven't even seen the host. I live right next door—” I pointed toward the invisible hedge in the shadows, “and this man Gatsby sent his driver over with a formal card.”

He gazed at me for a second, looking utterly astonished by my statement.

“I’m Gatsby,” he said suddenly.

“What!” I exclaimed. “Oh, I beg your pardon.”

“I thought you knew, old sport. I'm afraid I'm not a very good host.

We chatted briefly about a few damp, dismal villages in France. As it happened, he lived nearby, and he shared that he had recently purchased a hydroplane and was eager to test it tomorrow.

He stared at me for a moment, appearing completely bewildered by my words.

“I’m Gatsby,” he revealed abruptly.

“What!” I cried. “Oh, please forgive me.”

“I assumed you were aware, old sport. I fear I am not a very attentive host.”

“At what time?”

“Whenever it suits you best.”

I was just about to inquire his name when Jordan turned in our direction, smiling warmly.

“Are you enjoying yourself more now?” she asked.

“Much more.” I looked back at my companion. “This is a strange gathering for me. I haven't even met the host. I live just next door—” I gestured toward the dark, obscured shrubbery, “and this fellow Gatsby sent his chauffeur over with an official card.”

He stared at me quietly, appearing completely bewildered by what I had said.

“I’m Gatsby,” he said abruptly.

“What!” I gasped. “Oh, please forgive me.”

“I assumed you knew, old sport. I'm afraid I'm a rather dreadful host.”
saida_filtrada:
You look remarkably familiar,” he said, a pleasant smile on his face. “Weren’t you with the Third Division during the war?”

“Yes, indeed. I was in the Ninth Machine-gun Battalion.”

“And I served in the Seventh Infantry until June of nineteen-eighteen. I felt sure I had seen your face before.”

We briefly discussed a few damp, gloomy French villages. He mentioned that he lived just down the road, and happened to add that he had recently purchased a hydroplane and planned to test it tomorrow.

“Would you care to join me, old sport? We could take a trip along the Sound.”

“At what time?”

“Whenever it suits you best.”

I was just about to inquire his name when Jordan turned in our direction, smiling warmly.

“Are you enjoying yourself more now?” she asked.

“Much more.” I looked back at my companion. “This is a strange gathering for me. I haven't even met the host. I live just next door—” I gestured toward the dark, obscured shrubbery, “and this fellow Gatsby sent his chauffeur over with an official card.”

He stared at me quietly, appearing completely bewildered by what I had said.

“I’m Gatsby,” he said abruptly.

“What!” I gasped. “Oh, please forgive me.”

“I assumed you knew, old sport. I'm afraid I'm a rather dreadful host.

saida_bruta:
“You seem incredibly recognizable,” he remarked, offering a warm, friendly grin. “Didn’t you serve in the Third Division during the conflict?”

“I did, in fact. I belonged to the Ninth Machine-gun Battalion.”

“And I was stationed with the Seventh Infantry up to June of nineteen-eighteen. I felt certain your face was familiar.”

We chatted shortly about some dreary, rain-soaked French towns. He noted that he resided immediately down the street, and chanced to mention he had just bought a hydroplane and intended to try it out tomorrow.

“Would you like to come along, old sport? We could go for a run around the Sound.”

“What hour?”

“Whatever works best for your schedule.”

I was on the verge of asking who he was when Jordan faced us, her expression bright.

“Are you having a better time now?” she inquired.

“Significantly better.” I turned back to my associate. “This is an unusual party for me. I’ve not yet encountered the host. I reside right next door—” I pointed toward the shadowy, overgrown hedges, “and this man Gatsby had his driver bring over a formal invitation.”

He gazed at me silently, looking entirely perplexed by my comment.

“I am Gatsby,” he stated suddenly.

“What!” I exclaimed. “Oh, I am deeply sorry.”

“I thought you were aware, old sport. I fear I make a rather terrible host.”

saida_bruta:
“You look remarkably familiar,” he said, flashing a warm, amiable smile. “Weren’t you with the Third Division in the war?”

“I was, indeed. I served in the Ninth Machine-gun Battalion.”

“And I was with the Seventh Infantry until June of nineteen-eighteen. I knew I recognized your face.”

We talked briefly about a few dreary, wet French villages. He mentioned that he lived just next door, and casually added that he had recently purchased a hydroplane and planned to test it tomorrow morning.

“Would you care to join me, old sport? We could take a spin along the Sound.”

“At what time?”

“Whenever it is most convenient for you.”

I was just about to ask his name when Jordan turned toward us, smiling pleasantly.

“Are you enjoying yourself more now?” she asked.

“Much more.” I addressed my companion once more. “This is a peculiar gathering for me. I haven’t even met the host yet. I live right next door—” I gestured toward the dark, tangled hedge, “and this fellow Gatsby sent his chauffeur over with an invitation.”

He stared at me for a moment, appearing completely baffled by my words.

“I’m Gatsby,” he said abruptly.

“What!” I cried. “Oh, please forgive me.”

“I thought you knew, old sport. I’m afraid I’m not a very good host.”
